beginner.takeaway: When you can attack two loose pieces, choose the move that defends one of them before making a pawn capture.

intermediate.coaching: I'd play Nc4. Your a4 push is understandable because it defends the bishop on b2, but it does not solve the main problem: Black’s queen on a5 is still attacking your rook on c1. Play Nc4 instead. The knight lands on c4, attacks the queen, and defends the rook on c1. If Black recaptures with Qxc4, you can recapture with axb4 and keep your rook active.
intermediate.takeaway: When an attacked piece is hanging, look for a move that attacks it while defending your loose piece.

beginner.coaching: I'd play gxf4. Be8 is understandable because it defends the pawn on g5, but it lets White keep the active pawn on f4. Play gxf4 instead: remove the hanging pawn first, then you can look at how your pieces are defended.
beginner.takeaway: When you have a hanging pawn, remove it before defending it with a side piece.

intermediate.coaching: I'd play gxf4. Be8 is understandable because it defends the rook on g8, but it does not solve the main problem: White’s e3 pawn is hanging. Play gxf4 instead. That removes the hanging pawn and also attacks e3, so White has to respond to pressure on two squares.
intermediate.takeaway: When an opponent’s hanging pawn is loose, first look for a clean capture that removes it while attacking another loose piece.

advanced.coaching: I'd play gxf4. Be8 is understandable because it defends the rook on h8, but it does not solve the main tension: White’s e3 pawn is hanging and your rook on g8 is defended by it. Play gxf4 instead. That removes the hanging pawn while also attacking the e3 pawn, so White has to respond to pressure on two targets instead of just one.
advanced.takeaway: When an opponent’s hanging pawn is loose, remove it first; if it is defended, use your active rook to attack it.
